The word no is the start of the negotiation. It's that simple. You have to get over your fear. We've all been conditioned to fear the word, but I promise that the word no is not the end of the negotiation. We say no is an automatic response and it's seldom means that someone has considered all of the facts and made a rational decision. Usually, it's a temporary decision that we use to maintain the status quo until we know what's coming. No one likes surprises, so we try to avoid them. The word no provides protection, so much so that it can become automatic. A great way to get over this initial resistance to surprises is to give your counterpart the right to say no right off the bat. In Jim Camps' book, Start With No, he calls it the right to veto. He observes that people will fight for their right to say no, but once you give them that right, your negotiating environment will instantly become more constructive and collaborative. Letting the other side know they are free and welcome to say no has an extremely positive impact on any negotiation. This means that you have to train yourself to not react negatively to hearing the word no. Listen to it as something other than rejection, then respond accordingly. You just need to see the word no in one of its much more accurate forms. Here's what they really mean by the word no. I'm not yet ready to agree. You are making me feel uncomfortable. I don't understand. I don't think I can afford to. I want something else. I need more information. I want to talk it over with someone else. After pausing, ask your counterpart some solution-based questions, or you can label their affect. For example, what about this doesn't work for you? What would you need to make it work? It seems like there's something here that bothers you. It seems like if we fix this, we could make a deal. What it comes down to is the universal need for autonomy. People will die for autonomy. We have a country founded on it. Tell me what society, mankind's history that was content and slavery people want to and need to feel in control. When you give someone permission to say no to your ideas, their emotions calm down and their ability to make effective decisions increases rapidly. Your counterpart can finally take a clear look at your proposal and make a rational decision. Remember, saying no is a way to feel protection. So by allowing them to say it, it gives you time to elaborate and pivot in order to convince them that to change your proposing is better than what's ahead of them otherwise. People have a need to say no, so don't just hope to hear it at some point. Let them just say it early. Great negotiators seek no because they know that's when the real negotiation begins.
